title: Lesson 380
transcript: Let's now talk about some risks associated with the acquisition of products and services. So when you're buying, if you're a company and you're buying new servers, new software, things like that, What are some of the risks that you have to think about? And then in a moment here, we'll talk about how you can mitigate those risks. So a few specific risks that you need to know about. First is product tampering. Product tampering is where a manufacturer makes some product. And then the baddie, the attacker, somehow gets their hands on that product and tampers with it, messes with it in some ways, alters or modifies the product in such a way that it's probably not going to be great for you, the consumer. They might install... Perfect example is they might install like a key logger inside of a keyboard. Or they might implant some chip in a firewall that allows them to get remote root access to that firewall once it's installed in the organization. So there's all sorts of, and those are pretty sophisticated attacks, there's simpler ones as well, but product tampering is where an attacker gets their hands on a correctly manufactured product and then they tamper with it in some way, they mess with it in some way. which is different from counterfeits. Counterfeits are where, it's not like this was a valid product that was then messed with. Counterfeits is where basically an invalid product is made. It's where the attacker makes a replica or an imitation of a genuine product. Usually to make it more cheaply, so it might be made with hazardous materials, it might not work properly, it might not last as long as it's supposed to. So there's a bunch of issues with cheap counterfeits, because they're typically not manufactured as well as the real thing. And that, in certain circumstances, can have pretty dire consequences. And then the final risk here is implants. And implants is actually a subset of product tampering. So what implants specifically means is the attacker is implanting something into a product to perform an unauthorized activity. And the example I gave of putting a key logger, of the attacker installing a key logger in a keyboard, that's the perfect example of an implant. So just remember that product tampering is a broader term where the attacker kind of messes with the product in various different ways. And an implant is a subset of that. An implant is specifically where the attacker is inserting some sort of unauthorized something into a product, like a keylogger into a keyboard. what are some ways that we can mitigate these risks? I mean, generally high level, we can use some of the techniques that we'll talk about throughout this course. Things like doing third party assist assessments, um, looking at service level, um, requirements, things like that. You know, making sure you have good processes in place to make sure you're acquiring proper products, that you trust your vendors, things like that. But some specific things that you need to know about, some specific risk mitigations. First one we'll talk about here is the silicone root of trust. And this is basically the idea that you can essentially build a unique identifier into a piece of hardware at the hardware level. So it's like literally built into the hardware is a unique identifier for, say, every microchip that comes off the manufacturing line. And then you can use this unique identifier to obviously uniquely identify the hardware. And there's no way to fake it. And one of the things that you can then do with the silicone root of trust is kind of one of the techniques you can use for it. you can build up what's often referred to as a secure boot process. So the hardware, for instance, will check to make sure that the firmware that's about to run is the valid firmware. And by the way, this often relies on something we're going to talk about in the cryptography section, which is digital signatures. Digital signatures allow you to verify the authenticity and the integrity of some software, of some code, some data. So what you can do with this sort of silicone root of trust is the hardware, before the hardware, like when you first power on a system, before the hardware will allow the firmware to run, the hardware will first check to say, hey, Is this actually the valid firmware? And by the way, we'll talk about what firmware is later on as well. But it'll check using the digital signature to say, hey, is this valid hardware? Authenticity means, you know, who something came from. It's proof of origin. So you could make sure that this firmware actually came from the right manufacturer. And integrity means you can make sure that the... code hasn't been modified in any way. So you can basically do this check before the firmware was allowed to run. And then before the firmware loads some software, the firmware will do the same thing. The firmware will check to make sure the software that's about to run has a valid digital signature, checking the authenticity and the integrity of, say, the operating system. And then once the operating system is loaded, it will do a check on any applications that want to run to make sure that those applications have valid digital signatures, authenticity, and integrity. And this is often, like I said, referred to as a secure boot process, where each step of the boot process is checking the next piece of software that's loading to say, hey, Is this valid? And you can build that upon the silicon root of trust. You can build very secure systems this way that will only ever run or hopefully only ever run valid software. Physically unclonable function. What this is about is that even when a manufacturer intends to manufacture completely identical, say, microchips, there are still going to be subtle variations between them at the atomic level. And you can use these subtle variations to... to help you generate unique encryption keys, say, that are just associated with that particular bit of hardware. So this is what's known as a physically unclonable function. Each device, just based on the slight variations in how it's manufactured, can be a way of uniquely identifying it. And there's no way to fake that. That's why it's known as a physically unclonable function. And then the final idea here of limiting the risks associated with what you're acquiring is having a software bill of materials. Any application that you acquire nowadays has been built with functions, libraries from different sources. and so you can basically do an inventory of that where you're basically checking to make sure that you've understood what all the libraries are that are used who they've come from and and make sure that they're valid and so you could do that if you're acquiring some piece of software you could look to see okay well you know vendor wrote this part but they use this library and they use these functions who wrote those supply chain management this is basically like secured supply supply chain management understanding where your software came from and do you trust where it came from and how do you trust where it came from if you know like open source libraries are being used how do you make sure that those are valid and so forth so that's kind of the idea of a software bill of materials it's a comprehensive list of components libraries modules that are used in a piece of software and you understand where they all came from and you're confident in or at least you've understood the risks associated with each components and you've got controls in place to mitigate those risks should they ever occur. So that's kind of the idea of the software bill of materials. It's basically supply chain management.
